reference.title: Code Confidence Pack
reference.description: A layered checklist for building confidence that code changes are correct, maintainable and production‑ready. Agents should select appropriate layers based on the repository type, risk profile and organisational requirements. Not all layers apply to every repository, but the agent must justify omitting a layer.

section.title: Layer 1 – Formatting
section.content: Ensure that code is formatted consistently. Use automated formatters such as Prettier for JavaScript/TypeScript, Stylelint with the GDS config for CSS/Sass, Black for Python, rustfmt for Rust, gofmt for Go, `spotless` or `google-java-format` for Java and `dotnet format` for .NET. The agent must run the formatter with `--check` or equivalent to ensure no drift.

section.title: Layer 2 – Linting
section.content: Linting catches anti‑patterns and common mistakes. Use ESLint (with `eslint-config-next` for Next.js projects), Stylelint (with `stylelint-config-gds`), Ruff or Flake8 for Python, Clippy for Rust, `go vet` for Go, Checkstyle and SpotBugs for Java, and Roslyn analyzers for .NET. Run lints in CI and locally via pre‑commit hooks to get fast feedback.

section.title: Layer 3 – Type and static analysis
section.content: Use type checkers and static analysis tools appropriate to the language: TypeScript’s `tsc --noEmit`, MyPy or Pyright for Python, Rust’s borrow checker, Go’s type system, and tools like `sonarqube` or `tsec`. For languages without a static type system, consider typed superset languages or runtime type checking libraries.

section.title: Layer 4 – Unit tests
section.content: Write unit tests that cover individual functions or classes. Use Jest/Vitest for JavaScript, Pytest for Python, Rust’s built‑in test harness, Go’s `go test`, JUnit for Java and `xunit` for .NET. Aim for high coverage on critical logic but avoid meaningless coverage thresholds.

section.title: Layer 5 – Contract tests
section.content: When the repository exposes an API, CLI or library interface, write contract tests that assert the shape of requests and responses. Use tools like JSON Schema validation, Pact for service contracts, Snapshots for CLI output, and API spec generators. Fail the pipeline if contracts change without an ADR or major version bump.

section.title: Layer 6 – Integration tests
section.content: Verify how components interact with each other and with external systems (databases, queues, caches). Use test databases and containerised dependencies. Do not test against production services. Ensure integration tests run in CI and are isolated from each other.

section.title: Layer 7 – End‑to‑end tests
section.content: For user‑facing applications, simulate user flows using Playwright, Cypress or Selenium. Include accessibility checks (e.g., Playwright’s `expect(page).toHaveNoAccessibilityIssues()`). Keep these tests targeted and avoid flakiness. Run them on every PR or nightly depending on runtime.

section.title: Layer 8 – Accessibility
section.content: Use automated tools like axe‑core and Pa11y to detect accessibility issues. The DWP manual notes that axe‑core and Pa11y combined find roughly 35 % of issues; manual testing and user research are still required. Run automated accessibility tests in CI, and schedule manual audits before major releases. Document any accessibility defects and mitigation plans.

section.title: Layer 9 – Visual regression
section.content: Use tools like Percy or Chromatic for React components to catch unintended visual changes. Capture baseline screenshots and compare them against future changes. Review differences manually before merging.

section.title: Layer 10 – Performance and budgets
section.content: Establish performance budgets (e.g., maximum page load times, throughput, memory usage) and write benchmarks or smoke tests to enforce them. Use Lighthouse, WebPageTest or `node --prof` for web apps, `pytest-benchmark` for Python, `criterion` for Rust, `go test -bench` for Go, JMH for Java and BenchmarkDotNet for .NET. Fail the pipeline if budgets are exceeded, and investigate regressions.

section.title: Layer 11 – Security and supply chain
section.content: Use tools like CodeQL, Bandit, ESLint security plugins, Trivy, snyk and OWASP dependency check to discover vulnerabilities. Keep dependencies up to date using Dependabot. Deny the pipeline if secrets are committed or if critical vulnerabilities are present. For infrastructure code (e.g., Terraform), use tools like tfsec or Checkov.

section.title: Layer 12 – Documentation quality
section.content: Build documentation as part of CI. Use Sphinx or MkDocs for Python projects, JSDoc or Docusaurus for JS projects, Rust’s built‑in doc generator, Go’s `godoc`, JavaDoc for Java, and DocFX for .NET. Run markdownlint, vale or alex to lint prose, and link checkers to ensure references work. Fail the pipeline if docs build fails or broken links exist.

section.title: Layer 13 – Release confidence
section.content: Before tagging a release, run smoke tests in an environment that mimics production. Generate changelogs and release notes from commit messages and ensure version numbers follow semantic versioning. Produce SBOMs and cryptographic signatures for release artefacts. Define rollback plans and verify that release metrics (e.g., error rates, performance) are within acceptable ranges.
